section.title: Divorce, Children, and the Rich Young Man
section.sectionRationale: On the road toward Jerusalem, Jesus fields a test about divorce and recalls marriage to God's creation design. He welcomes the children the disciples would turn away, and He meets a rich young man whose wealth keeps him from the kingdom, teaching that salvation is impossible for man but possible with God.
section.thesis: The kingdom belongs to those who receive it like little children and cannot be entered by human achievement or wealth; God's design for marriage is lifelong union, and only God can save, richly rewarding all who leave everything to follow Jesus.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the question about divorce and God's design for marriage; Jesus blessing the little children; the rich young man who goes away sorrowful; and the danger of riches with the promise of reward for those who follow.

verses.text: When Jesus had finished saying these things, He left Galilee and went into the region of Judea beyond the Jordan.
Large crowds followed Him, and He healed them there.
Then some Pharisees came and tested Him by asking, “Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife for any reason?”
Jesus answered, “Have you not read that from the beginning the Creator ‘made them male and female,’
and said, ‘For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh’?
So they are no longer two, but one flesh. Therefore what God has joined together, let man not separate.”
“Why then,” they asked, “did Moses order a man to give his wife a certificate of divorce and send her away?”
Jesus replied, “Moses permitted you to divorce your wives because of your hardness of heart. But it was not this way from the beginning.
Now I tell you that whoever divorces his wife, except for {{porneia|sexual immorality}}, and marries another woman commits adultery.”
His disciples said to Him, “If this is the case between a man and his wife, it is better not to marry.”
“Not everyone can accept this word,” He replied, “but only those to whom it has been given.

For there are eunuchs who were born that way; others were made that way by men; and still others live like eunuchs for the sake of the kingdom of heaven. The one who can accept this should accept it.”
Then little children were brought to Jesus for Him to place His hands on them and pray for them. And the disciples rebuked those who brought them.
But Jesus said, “Let the little children come to Me, and do not hinder them! For the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these.”
And after He had placed His hands on them, He went on from there.
Just then a man came up to Jesus and inquired, “Teacher, what good thing must I do to obtain eternal life?”
“Why do you ask Me about what is good?” Jesus replied. “There is only One who is good. If you want to enter life, keep the commandments.”
“Which ones?” the man asked. Jesus answered, “‘Do not murder, do not commit adultery, do not steal, do not bear false witness,
honor your father and mother, and love your neighbor as yourself.’”
“All these I have kept,” said the young man. “What do I still lack?”
Jesus told him, “If you want to be {{teleios|perfect}}, go, sell your possessions and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow Me.”
When the young man heard this, he went away in sorrow, because he had great wealth.

units.label: Riches, and reward for the last
units.note: impossible for man, possible with God

verses.text: Then Jesus said to His disciples, “Truly I tell you, it is hard for a rich man to enter the kingdom of heaven.
Again I tell you, it is easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God.”
When the disciples heard this, they were greatly astonished and asked, “Who then can be saved?”
Jesus looked at them and said, “With man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible.”
“Look,” Peter replied, “we have left everything to follow You. What then will there be for us?”

Jesus said to them, “Truly I tell you, in the {{palingenesia|renewal}} of all things, when the Son of Man sits on His glorious throne, you who have followed Me will also sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel.
And everyone who has left houses or brothers or sisters or father or mother or wife or children or fields for the sake of My name will receive a hundredfold and will inherit eternal life.
But many who are first will be last, and the last will be first.

porneia.gloss: Sexual immorality, unfaithfulness of a broad kind. It is the exception clause in Jesus' teaching on divorce; its precise scope is debated, but it names a grave sexual breach of the one-flesh union that Jesus alone allows as a ground for divorce.

teleios.gloss: Complete, whole, mature, perfect. Jesus tells the young man that being teleios is not another achievement to add but the surrender of the one idol, his wealth, that his heart truly serves; wholeness is found in following Christ, not in self-made righteousness.

palingenesia.gloss: Rebirth, regeneration, the renewal of all things. It names the coming restoration of creation when the Son of Man reigns on His glorious throne, the age in which Christ's followers share His reward.

basic.context: Heading toward Jerusalem, Jesus is tested about divorce. He goes back to creation: God made marriage a lifelong one-flesh union, and Moses' permission to divorce was a concession to hard hearts. He welcomes little children when the disciples try to keep them away, saying the kingdom belongs to such. A rich young man asks how to gain eternal life; when Jesus tells him to sell all and follow, he leaves grieving. Jesus warns how hard riches make entry, but with God even that is possible, and He promises great reward to those who leave all to follow Him.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the three scenes together: they all expose what we cling to instead of the kingdom, whether a lax view of marriage, adult self-importance, or wealth. Jesus grounds marriage in creation, not merely in law. The rich man's story is not a general command to sell everything but a probe of the heart's true master. Salvation, Jesus says, is God's impossible-for-man work.
basic.originalLanguages: The debated exception clause turns on porneia, sexual immorality. Jesus tells the young man that to be teleios, whole, he must give up his idol and follow. And He promises reward in the palingenesia, the renewal of all things, when the Son of Man reigns.
basic.typology: Jesus recalling the creation of marriage points to the ultimate marriage, Christ and His church. The renewal of all things fulfills the prophetic hope of a restored creation under the reign of the Son of Man.
basic.christ: Jesus speaks with the Creator's authority over marriage, welcomes the little and the least, and exposes every rival to Himself in the human heart. He is the good treasure worth more than all wealth, the one worth leaving everything to follow, and the King who will reign in the renewed world.
basic.god: God is the author of marriage, the welcomer of children, the only One who is good, and the only One who can save. What is impossible for people, He makes possible, and He richly rewards those who forsake all for His name.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 1 and 2 give the creation of marriage Jesus cites. Malachi 2 declares God hates divorce. Mark 10 and Luke 18 parallel these scenes. Revelation 21 depicts the renewal of all things Jesus promises.
basic.oneStory: As Jesus nears Jerusalem, He calls people to receive the kingdom as helpless children and to release every idol, marriage held cheap, self-importance, and wealth, that competes with God. Salvation is impossible for man but possible with God, and those who leave all to follow the King will share His reign in the renewed world. The road to the cross is the road that gains everything.

context.literary: Chapter 19 opens the journey to Jerusalem with Matthew's closing-of-discourse formula. Three encounters, on marriage, children, and wealth, each test what a person will hold onto against the kingdom, and they build toward the reversal saying, the first will be last, which the next chapter's parable of the vineyard illustrates. The rich man's failure contrasts sharply with the disciples who have left all.
context.historical: Rabbis debated the grounds for divorce: the school of Shammai allowed it only for sexual sin, the school of Hillel for almost any cause, which lies behind the Pharisees' any reason. A certificate of divorce came from Deuteronomy 24. The camel through a needle's eye is a vivid image of impossibility. Children had little social status, making the disciples' rebuff unsurprising and Jesus' welcome striking.
context.canonical: Jesus roots marriage in Genesis 1:27 and 2:24, treating the creation order as binding. His teaching stands with Malachi 2:16 against casual divorce. The welcome of children develops the childlike-humility theme of chapter 18. The renewal of all things links to Isaiah 65, Acts 3:21, and Revelation 21, the restored creation under the reigning Son of Man of Daniel 7.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal God's creation design for marriage against a culture of easy divorce, to show that the kingdom is received in childlike dependence, and to expose wealth and self-righteousness as barriers only God can overcome, with rich reward for those who follow.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The teaching on marriage and the danger of riches is prescriptive. The command to the rich young man to sell everything is a specific diagnosis of his particular idol, not a universal rule, though it prescribes for all that nothing may rival Christ. The promise of reward describes God's generosity to all who forsake all.

debates.question: What does Jesus permit regarding divorce and remarriage in the exception clause?

views.label: Divorce and remarriage for sexual immorality
views.case: Jesus allows an exception, except for sexual immorality (porneia); where a spouse gravely breaks the one-flesh bond by sexual sin, the innocent party may divorce, and many hold the freedom to remarry follows, since the marriage has been fundamentally violated.

views.label: A high view that minimizes any remarriage
views.case: Others stress that Jesus' whole thrust is the permanence of marriage against the lax Hillelite view; the exception is narrow, and some traditions read it as permitting separation while remarriage remains restricted, guarding the one-flesh union God joined.

debates.bearing: All sides agree Jesus reasserts God's creation design of lifelong marriage against casual divorce; the exception is real but narrow, and the pastoral aim is to honor the covenant, not to widen the exits.

study.originalLanguages: Against a culture that divorced for any reason, Jesus grounds marriage in creation and allows only the narrow exception of porneia, sexual immorality, a grave breach of the one-flesh bond. To the rich young man, who has kept the commandments outwardly, Jesus says that to be teleios, whole and complete, he must surrender the one thing his heart truly serves, his wealth, and follow. And to those who have left everything, Jesus promises a share in His reign in the palingenesia, the renewal or rebirth of all things, when the Son of Man sits on His glorious throne. In each scene the real question is what a person will cling to instead of the kingdom.

typology.fulfillment: The union of Christ and His church, the marriage that creation's marriage foreshadows, Ephesians 5:31-32.
The restored creation under the reigning Son of Man, promised by the prophets and fulfilled in the new heavens and earth, Isaiah 65:17, Revelation 21:1-5.
The enduring inheritance Christ gives, surpassing all earthly wealth, 1 Peter 1:4.

study.christ: Jesus speaks as the Creator's voice over marriage, calling His people back to God's design. He gathers the children the world overlooks, declaring the kingdom theirs, and He puts His finger on the idol that keeps the rich man out, offering Himself as a treasure worth more than everything sold to gain Him. He is honest that salvation is impossible for man, and He points to God with whom all things are possible, for He Himself is the One who will do the impossible at the cross. And He is the enthroned Son of Man of the coming renewal, who rewards a hundredfold all who leave houses and family and fields for His name.
study.god: The God of this chapter is the Creator who instituted marriage as a lifelong one-flesh bond and who joins husband and wife, so that divorce tears what He has united. He is the only One who is good, the standard the young man's question implies. He welcomes the small and dependent, and He alone can save, doing what is impossible for man. Far from being in anyone's debt, He is lavishly generous, promising a hundredfold and eternal life to all who forsake everything for His Son, and overturning human rankings so that the last will be first.

crossReferences.note: A man leaves and cleaves and the two become one flesh, the design Jesus cites.
God's declared displeasure at faithless divorce, behind Jesus' appeal to the beginning.
The parallel account of the rich man and the reward for those who leave all.
The renewal of all things Jesus promises, the new heavens and earth.

study.oneStory: Nearing Jerusalem, Jesus calls for hearts that receive the kingdom like children and release every rival to God, whether a marriage held cheap, self-importance, or wealth. The rich man cannot let go and walks away sorrowful, while the disciples who left all are promised a share in the renewed world. Salvation is impossible for man but possible with God, who will accomplish it at the cross and consummate it when the Son of Man reigns. The way of losing all for Christ is the way that inherits everything.

quiz.q: On what basis does Jesus answer the question about divorce?

options.text: God's creation design: marriage is a lifelong one-flesh union that man must not separate.
options.note: Verses 4-6 appeal to creation, not merely to Moses' concession.

options.text: The certificate of divorce that Moses commanded.
options.note: Jesus addresses Moses' permission, but calls it a concession to hard hearts, not the original design.

options.text: Whatever the culture of the day found acceptable.
options.note: Jesus rejects the lax any-reason view, appealing to God's design instead.

options.text: A rule that divorce is always forbidden with no exception.
options.note: Jesus names a narrow exception; the point is God's design, not an absolute ban with no exception.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus welcome the little children?

options.text: Because the kingdom of heaven belongs to those who receive it like them.
options.note: Verse 14 makes the children a picture of childlike reception of the kingdom.

options.text: Because He wanted to bless as many people as possible.
options.note: He does bless them, but the reason given is that the kingdom belongs to such as these.

options.text: Because children had earned a place by their innocence.
options.note: The point is dependent reception, not earned merit or innocence.

options.text: Because the disciples had asked Him to bless them.
options.note: The disciples rebuked those bringing the children; Jesus overruled them.

quiz.q: Why did the rich young man go away sorrowful?

options.text: His wealth was the idol he would not surrender to follow Jesus.
options.note: Verses 21-22 expose his great wealth as the one thing he could not release.

options.text: Jesus asked more of him than of anyone else.
options.note: Jesus named his particular idol; the deeper issue is that his heart served wealth, not the size of the demand.

options.text: He had failed to keep any of the commandments.
options.note: He claimed to have kept them; his lack was an undivided heart, not total failure.

options.text: Jesus refused to tell him how to gain eternal life.
options.note: Jesus told him plainly; he refused the call, not the other way around.

quiz.q: What does Jesus teach about who can be saved?

options.text: Salvation is impossible for man but possible with God.
options.note: Verse 26 grounds salvation in God's power, not human ability.

options.text: Only the poor can be saved, not the rich.
options.note: Riches are a serious danger, but the point is that God can save even a rich person; salvation is God's work.

options.text: Anyone can save themselves by keeping the commandments.
options.note: The young man kept them outwardly yet lacked; salvation is God's doing, not self-achievement.

options.text: No one can ever be saved at all.
options.note: With God all things are possible; salvation is real, though only God can accomplish it.

quiz.q: What does Jesus promise those who leave everything to follow Him?

options.text: A hundredfold now and eternal life, sharing His reign in the renewal of all things.
options.note: Verses 28-29 promise abundant reward and a share in the coming renewal.

options.text: That they will avoid all hardship in this life.
options.note: Reward is promised, but not an escape from hardship; the reward includes the age to come.

options.text: That they have earned salvation by their sacrifice.
options.note: The reward is God's generous gift, not a wage earned; salvation remains His work.

options.text: Nothing, since following Jesus brings only loss.
options.note: Jesus explicitly promises a hundredfold and eternal life.

questions: Jesus rooted marriage in God's design from the beginning. How does seeing marriage as something God joins, rather than a contract we manage, shape the way you honor it?
The kingdom belongs to those who come like children. Where is adult self-sufficiency keeping you from receiving God's grace with open, empty hands?
Jesus named the one thing the young man could not release. What is the possession, comfort, or security you would find hardest to give up for Him?
Salvation is impossible for man but possible with God. How does that free you from both despair about others and pride about yourself?
Those who leave all for Christ receive a hundredfold. Where is fear of loss holding you back from a step of obedience He is asking?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscripts add and he who marries a divorced woman commits adultery, as in Matthew 5:32; the exception, except for sexual immorality, renders the Greek porneia, whose scope is discussed in the study.
Some manuscripts read Good Teacher and Why do you call Me good, as in Mark and Luke; the BSB follows the reading Why do you ask Me about what is good, with the same force, that God alone is good.
The word rendered the renewal of all things may also be translated the regeneration; it names the coming restoration of creation when the Son of Man reigns on His glorious throne.
